title: ⭐⭐ Review: Stance, Tone and Vocabulary in Context

questions.prompt:
Read: "The committee's report is, to put it charitably, incomplete."
What does the writer imply?

options.text: The writer is neutral and simply describing the report's length.
The writer is being generous — the report is actually quite good.
The writer praises the committee for its transparency.
The report is worse than merely incomplete — 'charitably' signals the writer is understating the criticism.

questions.explanation: 'To put it charitably' is an understatement signal: the writer is saying this is the kindest possible description, implying the reality is harsher — the report may be seriously flawed, not merely incomplete. Option (b) misreads charitably as genuine praise. Option (a) ignores the evaluative force of 'charitably'. Option (c) invents a positive interpretation not supported by the text.

questions.prompt:
Read: "The data may suggest a correlation, though causation remains, of course, entirely unproven."
What is the writer's stance?

options.text: Cautious and sceptical — hedging language signals a tentative, qualified view.
Conciliatory — the writer is agreeing with an opposing argument.
Assertive — the writer is confident that causation has been established.
Ironic — the writer believes causation is obvious but is being sarcastic.

questions.explanation: 'May suggest' (hedging modal + verb), 'though' (concessive), and 'entirely unproven' all signal caution and scepticism. The writer is presenting a tentative, carefully qualified view — not asserting anything firmly. Option (c) is the opposite: assertiveness would use 'demonstrates' or 'proves'. Option (d) invents irony where none is signalled. Option (b) misreads the hedge as conciliation toward an opposing party.

questions.prompt:
Read: "Her tenacity — her refusal to abandon the project despite repeated setbacks — ultimately secured its success."
What does 'tenacity' mean here?

options.text: Her strategic approach to planning
Her ability to attract funding
Her talent for solving technical problems
Her persistent refusal to give up

questions.explanation: The synonym clue is built into the sentence: 'tenacity' is immediately glossed as 'her refusal to abandon the project despite repeated setbacks' — a classic apposition/synonym structure. Options (a), (b), and (c) introduce meanings not supported by the surrounding definition.

questions.prompt:
Read: "The policy was praised by industry leaders. Such enthusiasm, however, was conspicuously absent among consumer groups."
What does 'such enthusiasm' refer to?

options.text: The industry leaders' praise for the policy
The government's confidence in the policy
The consumer groups' support for the policy
The general public's indifference to the policy

questions.explanation: 'Such enthusiasm' is a cohesive device — 'such + noun' refers back to a previously described quality. The enthusiasm described in the preceding sentence belongs to the industry leaders who praised the policy. The sentence then says this enthusiasm was absent among consumer groups — a contrast. Options (c) and (d) misread the referent. Option (b) introduces the government, which is not mentioned.

questions.prompt:
Read: "The author notes that economic growth 'trickled down' to lower-income groups."
What does the use of quotation marks around 'trickled down' suggest?

options.text: The author considers the trickle-down effect to be well established.
The author is distancing themselves from the phrase, implying scepticism about the claim.
The author invented this phrase and is introducing a new term.
The author is quoting a specific report.

questions.explanation: Quotation marks around a term (sometimes called 'scare quotes') signal that the writer is distancing themselves from it — often implying the term is contested, misleading, or used ironically. Here, putting 'trickled down' in quotes implies the author doubts this actually happened. Option (c) misreads scare quotes as a neologism marker. Option (d) would require an attributed source. Option (a) is the opposite of what the scare quotes signal.

questions.prompt:
Read: "Three economists were consulted. All three warned of severe risks. Their warnings were noted."
What does 'were noted' most likely imply in this context?

options.text: The warnings were found to be inaccurate.
The warnings were the main finding of the report.
The warnings were recorded but probably ignored or disregarded.
The warnings were acted upon immediately and decisively.

questions.explanation: In formal or bureaucratic contexts, 'were noted' is often a passive understatement implying that something was acknowledged without any consequential action being taken — the implication is that the warnings were filed away rather than acted on. This is a common English understatement pattern. Option (d) would be expressed more actively ('acted upon', 'implemented'). Option (a) introduces inaccuracy not implied by the text. Option (b) overstates the role of the warnings.
